questions.question: A teammate submits a 50-line bugfix PR. You glance at it, see no obvious issues, and approve with 'LGTM' in under 2 minutes. What is the primary risk of this approach?
options: The CI pipeline might not have finished yet
You may have missed a subtle correctness or security issue because you didn't read carefully enough
The author might feel their work isn't valued
The PR will take longer to merge
questions.explanation: LGTM-without-reading is the most dangerous review anti-pattern. Every major postmortem at tech companies has involved a rubber-stamped PR. Even small changes can contain subtle bugs, security issues, or logic errors that only emerge from careful reading.

questions.question: During a code review, you notice a teammate's PR uses string concatenation to build a SQL query with user input. What type of review comment should this be, and how should you phrase it?
options: [nit] — 'Consider using parameterized queries instead of string concatenation'
[suggestion] — 'I prefer parameterized queries, but it's up to you'
[blocking] — 'This could be a SQL injection vulnerability. User input must be parameterized, not concatenated into the query string. Here's the pattern we use: [link]'
[question] — 'Is string concatenation safe here?'
questions.explanation: Security vulnerabilities are always blocking — the PR cannot merge until addressed. The comment should explain why (injection risk), provide a concrete alternative (parameterized queries), and link to the team's standard pattern. Questions and suggestions understate the severity; nits are for style preferences.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of an effective review comment?
options: 'This code is wrong.'
'What happens when `items` is empty? I think this will throw an IndexError because the loop assumes at least one element.'
'I would have written this differently.'
'LGTM, just fix the trailing whitespace.'
questions.explanation: Effective reviews ask questions, explain why, and point to concrete issues. Option B identifies a specific edge case, explains the consequence, and invites the author to fix or explain — without being confrontational. Options A and C are vague and unhelpful. Option D is bike-shedding: focusing on whitespace while potentially missing real issues.

questions.question: Your team's PRs keep growing past 500 lines, and reviews are getting shallow. What is the best strategy to improve review quality?
options: Require more reviewers per PR so at least one will read it carefully
Add a longer PR description template so reviewers have more context
Split large PRs into smaller, focused changes (under 400 lines) and use stacked PRs for dependent changes
Increase the required wait time before a PR can be merged, giving reviewers more time
questions.explanation: Small PRs get better reviews — this is consistently observed across Google, Microsoft, and Stripe. A 50-line diff gets line-by-line attention; a 500-line diff gets a skim. Stacked PRs let you decompose large changes into reviewable increments (data model → read path → write path). More reviewers doesn't fix the root cause. Longer descriptions help but don't offset diff size. Waiting longer just delays without improving depth.

questions.question: During a review, three team members are debating whether to name a variable `idx` or `index` while a race condition on line 30 goes unmentioned. What anti-pattern does this illustrate, and what's the fix?
options: Rubber-stamping — fix by requiring at least one specific observation per approval
Bike-shedding — fix by delegating naming to style guides/linters, and redirect human review time to correctness, security, and design
Review blockade — fix by setting a 24-hour SLA for first-round review
Nitpicking — fix by removing all review comments that aren't about bugs
questions.explanation: Bike-shedding is spending disproportionate time on trivial decisions while overlooking serious issues. Named after Parkinson's observation that committees spend more time on a bike shed than a nuclear plant. The fix is to let formatters and linters handle style, and reserve human review for things machines can't check: correctness, design, and security.

questions.question: According to Google's engineering practices, what is the correct order of priorities when reviewing code?
options: Readability → Design → Correctness
Performance → Security → Correctness
Correctness → Design → Readability
Style → Tests → Documentation
questions.explanation: Google explicitly prioritizes correctness first, then design, then readability. This ordering is deliberate: a well-formatted bug is still a bug. Correctness (does the code do what it's supposed to?) always trumps style and even design elegance.
